: NATIONAL SECURITY COUNCIL
SECRET WASHINGTON, D.C. 20506 21075
Meeting of the Deputies Committee
DATE: May 31, 1989
TIME: 11:00-12:00 noon
PLACE: Situation Room
SUBJECT: NSR 14 Papers on START and Defense & Space
PARTICIPANTS: 7
Vice President's Office White House
Carnes Lord Robert Gates
State NSC
Allan Holmes
Edward Rowny Arnold Kanter
Richard Burt William Tobey
James Timbie
Defense
Paul Wolfowitz
Stephen Hadley
Energy
Henson Moore
Vic Alessi
ACDA
George Murphy
JCS
H. T. Johnston
OMB
William Diefenderfer
Minutes
Mr. Gates began by saying the purpose of the meeting is to ensure
that the papers fairly represent the views of agencies and
thoroughly discuss the options. He said he would like to begin
the Defense & Space paper on activities during a period of
non-withdrawal from the ABM Treaty. There, he said that he
understood that all agencies except the JCS favored our current
position. As he understood it, the JCS are concerned that our
SREREP

do(0-
SEGREF F4-ue
C& 4le(ra

Py -- =
SEGRE? 2
Defense & Space position could constrain U.S. activities, while
placing no limits on the Soviets. Mr. Gates concluded by asking
if his understanding of agency positions was correct. (2)
Amb. Holmes said that the State Department favored going back to
Geneva with the existing U.S. position, but that more work needs
to be done. Specifically, given the importance of "Brilliant
Pebbles" is the system based on "other physical principles," and
is implicit Soviet acceptance of our right to test OPP systems in
space sufficient for Senate ratification? ()
Amb. Wolfowitz said that OSD was happy with the paper and favored
Option 1 (the current U.S. position). He also held open the
possibility that we may wish to seek clearer Soviet acceptance of
U.S. rights at a later date. (f)
Mr. Moore said that Energy was also happy with the paper, and
favored the current position. He continued that "Brilliant
Pebbles" research is very promising. (J)
Mr. Murphy said that he favored Option 1 (the current U.S.
position). (f)
Gen. Johnston said that we need to clarify ambiguity in the ABM
Treaty regime in order to attain Senate ratification and to allay
UK and French concerns. (jf)
Mr. Gates asked wouldn't the JCS position that we should seek the
right to all ABM testing in space cause us to renegotiate the ABM
Treaty, something we have been reluctant to do? (P)
Mr. Timbie observed that the U.S. initiative on space-based
sensors would require amendment of the ABM Treaty. g
Gen. Johnston observed that the ABM Treaty supports the essence
of the sensor initiative. )
Amb. Rowny said that amending the ABM Treaty to allow all
space-based ABM testing would open a Pandora's box. (#4
Mr. Gates asked if all were agreed that the U.S. initiative on
space-based sensors should be retained, and received general
assent. He then moved the discussion to the second Defense &
Space paper on activities after the non-withdrawal period. He
again characterized agency views, stating that all except the JCS
favor retention of the current U.S. position, and noting that the
JCS are concerned that the U.S. position could imply a date
certain for termination of the ABM Treaty. (%
Amb. Holmes said that State Department is comfortable with the
tei

|: SFeect
SECREP 3
existing position. He said that there would be a maelstrom of
Congressional and Allied reaction to withdrawal from the ABM
Treaty, and that therefore the current U.S. position was
necessary to redress asymmetries in U.S. and Soviet options
because the Soviet Union would not face such pressures. (Z)
Gen. Johnston said that we should not let the Soviets have a date
certain for the end of the ABM Treaty. He said he felt that Art.
III of the current U.S. draft treaty did that. (f)
Amb. Wolfowitz said that OSD likes the current position. He said
it recognizes the basic asymmetry between U.S. and Soviet legal
and political constraints. He said that the Soviets can leave
the ABM Treaty any time they wish, while the U.S. is constrained
in its actions. )
Amb. Holmes said that perhaps we should look at ways of dealing
with this in the future. (J)
Mr. Moore. said the Energy joins the consensus in favor of the
present position. ()
Mr. Murphy and Mr. Diefenderfer agreed. (7g
Gen. Johnston offered a written substitute for the JCS rationale
for their preferred option in the Defense & Space paper on
activities during a period of non-withdrawal from the ABM Treaty.
(Z)
Mr. Gates then turned to START issues. He said there are three
issues: the Strategic Nuclear Delivery Vehicle (SNDV) limit;
ICBM warhead sublimits; and heavy missiles. On the first issue,
Mr. Gates acknowledged that he would have to speak with General
Scowcroft, as there was interagency consensus that we should
retain the limit of 1600. On the ICBM sublimit, the question was
whether to pursue a sublimit of 3,000 or 3,300. 4)
Amb. Holmes said State did not wish to denigrate the coin and
that we should stick with 3,000, especially given that we do not
know what we would want in return. B)
Amb. Wolfowitz agreed saying the most pressing problem is that of
ICBM first strike potential, and that before we move to a higher
number we should be clear on what we want in return. )
Gen. Johnston suggested that we should negotiate to see if we can
get something, but not give concessions away. 6-4)
Mr. Murphy said that we should stick with the present position,
noting that if we don't know what we want, we probably won't get
ae

SBCREP-- a
it. (~
Mr. Diefenderfer agreed. (jg)
Amb. Burt said the practical difference between 3000 and 3300 is
negligible, but we should not change now. (sf
Mr. Gates then summarized four options for heavy ICBMs:
equality through elimination over seven years or longer; the
current approach of banning flight testing, production, and
modernization; banning flight testing only; and permitting flight
testing and limited modernization in return for equal U.S. legal
rights to deploy heavy missiles. (9)
Amb. Holmes said we should stick with the current position -- no
new missiles, no modernization, no testing. We may wish to
consider private probing on the side to see if the Soviets are
willing to go beyond 50 percent reductions in return for enough
testing for reliability, but not for modernization. 4)
Amb. Wolfowitz said we should stay with the current position. He
said he would favor option 1, banning heavy missiles, but was
unsure of what we would have to pay for such a ban, or what it
would be worth. ()
Mr. Gates asked whether anyone seriously favored the fourth
option, equal legal rights to deploy heavy missiles, given that
the U.S. would be unlikely ever to exercise such rights. ¥
Amb. Burt said he did not share Mr. Gates' view. He said that if
we can SS-18 two bad things could happen. First, we might have
to make a significant concession. Second, five to ten years from
now we might face a formidable Soviet hard target threat from
SS-24s and SLBMs. Nonetheless, he said he would like the
flexibility to talk to the Soviets about limits that go beyond
those presently proposed, but not to discuss this publicly. 2)
Mr. Gates observed that if we take a treaty to the Senate arguing
we have fulfilled the Jackson Amendment because we have a de jure
right to heavy missiles but no intention to deploy such missiles,
we will be told to get stuffed. ()
Gen. Johnston said the JCS favor Option 4 and would give the
Negotiator authority to try to get something in return. He
acknowledged that the Jackson Amendment might be of concern. B)
Mr. Moore said he favored the status quo, and that we should
change only if we have good reason to.
Mr. Murphy said he was against standing pat, because it would
SSERBP

rae 0
SBERET 5
guarantee Soviet superiority. He asked, what would we have to
pay for zero heavy missiles? And, why did we give up our rights
to heavy missiles in the first place? He said he favored cutting
the Soviet limit to 77, vice 154, but that ACDA favored option 3.
Mr. Diefenderfer said no comment. (f
Mr. Timbie said that if the Soviets can keep their heavies, and
we cannot deploy them, the Senate would likely not ratify the
Treaty. v0
Amb. Rowny said that we should probe the Soviets to see if they
are willing to accept deeper cuts in heavy missiles but not pay
anything for the additional cuts. 2)
Amb. Wolfowitz observed that it is a miracle that the Soviets
appear to be unaware of U.S. contingency guidance on the subject,
that had been in effect since last year. v4
-- End of Meeting --

| NSR_14 START Review Paper Four -- Air Breathing Systems
: Purpose. To review U.S. positions on dealing with air breathing
| systems under START.
| Background. A fundamental premise underlying the U.S. approach
to START is that we must be capable of maintaining an adequate
deterrent under a START regime. In order to do so, and to
improve stability, the U.S. has consistently opposed tight constraints on ALCMs and other bomber weapons in START. The original U.S. position did not directly limit these systems, constraining them only indirectly through an overall limit on the
number of heavy bombers. If ALCMs and other bomber weapons are
to count under the 6,000 warhead limit in START, as currently
agreed, they must be heavily discounted in order to allow meeting
| present and START projected U.S. military requirements.
| Significant features of the current U.S. approach include::
-- Counting bombers which carry only gravity bombs and SRAMs as
"one" against both the 1600 SNDV and 6000 warhead limits.
-- Counting ALCM-carrying heavy bombers as one unit against the
1600 SNDV limit and 10 warheads against the 6000 warhead
limit regardless of actual load or capability.
-- Considering "ALCMs" to include only nuclear-armed airlaunched cruise missiles with ranges in excess of 1500
; kilometers.:
-- Rejecting any limits on long-range conventionally-armed
cruise missiles (LRCCM). Treating all currently existing
long-range air-to-surface cruise missiles as nuclear armed
while distinguishing future LRCCMs from treaty accountable
ALCMs through NTM.
-- Rejecting any limits on nondeployed ALCMS or on total ALCM
inventory.
-- Providing for a limited number of former heavy bombers (i.e.
conventional bombers) which would not count against either
the 1600 or 6000 limits.
-- Providing separate basing for the three classes of bombers
(ALCM-carrying heavy bombers, non-ALCM carrying heavy
bombers, former heavy bombers), with a limited on-site
inspection right for non-ALCM carrying heavy bombers of a
. type tested with treaty accountable ALCMs.

Discussion -- General. Because of the importance of this area
and the close interrelationship of all heavy bomber/ALCM positions, a comprehensive review is required. There is consensus
- that the United States should not alter its current rejection of
Soviet attempts to place sublimits on bomber weapons or to constrain ALCMs, e.g., to limit nondeployed ALCMs.
Discussion -- Counting Rules. For many years the United States
rejected placing any limits on ALCMs. The U.S. approach to ALCM
counting rules discounts ALCMs and heavily discounts non-ALCM
nuclear weapons on bombers. Some have raised the question of
whether discounting bomber weapons would in fact favor the
Soviets in the long run, given the asymmetries in air defenses.
Others believe that, while the Soviets appear to be well posi-~
tioned to exploit these counting rules, encouraging greater:
. Soviet reliance on air-breathing systems is a central goal of the
U.S. START position. Moreover, the U.S. requires these systems
for target coverage, while the Soviets do not.
The most common alternative counting rule is the so-called "as-
. equipped" approach, in which ALCM bombers are counted against the
6000 limit based on the maximum number of ALCMs they are capable
of carrying. This position, favored by the Soviets, would either
(a) require substantial modifications to U.S. bombers to limit
the number of ALCMs they could carry or (b) lead to unrealistically high attributions which would severely inhibit U.S.
flexibility. The Soviets claim that the U.S. attribution rule
provides the U.S. with an unfair advantage since future widebodied aircraft could carry dozens of cruise missiles but would
only be attributed with ten. Some favor attempting to meet this
stated Soviet concern by placing some upper limit on the ALCMcarrying capacity of individual aircraft. Others believe the
loss of flexibility and verification problems make it imprudent
to attempt to placate what they 'see as a spurious Soviet concern.
To move toward a more stabilizing defense posture, some believe
the U.S. could consider readopting its original position that
ALCMs and other bomber weapons will not be counted or limited
under START. This would entail -having limits only on ballistic
missile warheads and on strategic nuclear delivery systems.
Heavy bombers would continue to count against the latter limit
only. Others believe that this move would encourage competition
in air-breathing systems and, because of the Soviet lead in air
defenses, therefore would not be stabilizing. Moreover, this
move could incite strong Soviet condemnation as backtracking on a
key U.S. concession and, by reopening the Reykjavik bomber

the necessary high number of bomber weapons. On the other hand,
such a move would simplify U.S. obligations under the treaty,
since no distinctions would have to be made (or verified) regarding conventionally-armed versus nuclear-armed ALCMs, or heavy
bombers equipped for ALCMs vice those not so equipped.
A less radical move would be to return to the earlier U.S.
position that each heavy bomber equipped for ALCMs should count
as six warheads (instead of ten), regardless of the number
actually carried. While this position would not simplify the
treaty in any way, and could encounter Soviet opposition, it
could increase U.S. flexibility and confidence in meeting targeting requirements under START constraints.

as ALCMs under START. Although there are no current programs for
nuclear armed cruise missiles with ranges between 600 and 1500
kilometers, some believe there is a clear military requirement to
: protect the option to deploy such systems in the future. More-
